category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Model Paper 08/Part A - General Aptitude

info.text: Part A: General Aptitude (Q1–15). Marks: +2 correct, −0.5 incorrect.

questiontext.text: An alloy contains copper and zinc in the ratio 7:3. How much zinc must be added to 100 kg of the alloy to make the ratio 7:4?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 10 kg

Initial: copper = 70 kg, zinc = 30 kg. New ratio 7:4 means for 70 kg copper, zinc should be 40 kg. Zinc to be added = 40−30 = 10 kg.

name.text: P08 Q2 - Speed distance two cities

questiontext.text: A car travels from city A to city B at 60 km/h and returns at 40 km/h. What is the average speed for the entire journey?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) 48 km/h

Average speed = 2v₁v₂/(v₁+v₂) = 2×60×40/(60+40) = 4800/100 = 48 km/h.

name.text: P08 Q3 - Loss selling below CP

questiontext.text: If an article is sold for Rs. 240 at a loss of 20%, what was the cost price?
Mountain : Valley :: Peak : ?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) Trough

A valley is the lowest point relative to a mountain; a trough is the lowest point relative to a peak (in waves).

questiontext.text: A mother is 3 times as old as her daughter. After 12 years, she will be twice as old. What is the daughter's present age?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 12

Let daughter = d, mother = 3d. After 12: 3d+12 = 2(d+12) ⟹ 3d+12 = 2d+24 ⟹ d = 12.

name.text: P08 Q6 - Number series pattern cubes

questiontext.text: What is the next number in the series? 1, 8, 27, 64, 125, ?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (4) 216

The series is 1³, 2³, 3³, 4³, 5³, 6³ = 216.

questiontext.text: Three partners A, B, C start a business with Rs. 20000, Rs. 30000, and Rs. 50000 respectively. At the end of the year, the profit is Rs. 36000. What is C's share?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) Rs. 18000

Ratio = 20:30:50 = 2:3:5. Total parts = 10. C's share = (5/10)×36000 = Rs. 18000.

questiontext.text: A hemispherical bowl of radius 6 cm is filled with water. What is the volume of water? (Use π = 3.14)

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 452.16 cm³

Volume of hemisphere = (2/3)πr³ = (2/3)×3.14×216 = (2/3)×678.24 = 452.16 cm³.

questiontext.text: A bag contains 5 red, 4 blue, and 3 green marbles. If two marbles are drawn at random without replacement, what is the probability that both are red?
Mohan walks 10 km North, then 6 km East, then 10 km South. How far is he from his starting point and in which direction?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 6 km East

10 km North and 10 km South cancel. Net: 6 km East from starting point.

questiontext.text: Ravi's mother's brother's son is Ravi's:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) Cousin

Mother's brother = maternal uncle. Uncle's son = Ravi's maternal cousin.

questiontext.text: The largest 3-digit number that is exactly divisible by 12, 18, and 24 is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (4) 936

LCM(12,18,24) = 72. Largest 3-digit multiple of 72: 999/72 = 13.87... So 13×72 = 936.

name.text: P08 Q13 - Odd one out shapes

questiontext.text: Which is the odd one out? Square, Rectangle, Triangle, Rhombus

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) Triangle

Square, Rectangle, and Rhombus are all four-sided quadrilaterals. Triangle has three sides — it is the odd one out.

questiontext.text: How many 4-digit numbers can be formed using the digits 1, 2, 3, 5 without repetition, such that the number is divisible by 5?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 6

For divisibility by 5, the units digit must be 5. Remaining 3 places can be filled with 1,2,3 in 3! = 6 ways.

questiontext.text: If all A are B and no B is C, then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) No A is C

All A ⊆ B, and B ∩ C = ∅. Since all A are in B and B has no overlap with C, A has no overlap with C. So no A is C.

category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Model Paper 08/Part B - Core Mathematics

info.text: Part B: Core Mathematics (Q16–40). Marks: +3 correct, −1 incorrect.

name.text: P08 Q16 - Metric space total boundedness

questiontext.text: A metric space \((X,d)\) is totally bounded if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) for every ε>0, X can be covered by finitely many balls of radius ε

Total boundedness: for every ε>0, there exists a finite ε-net (finite cover by ε-balls). A metric space is compact iff it is complete AND totally bounded.

answer.text: for every \(\varepsilon>0\), \(X\) can be covered by finitely many balls of radius \(\varepsilon\)
every sequence has a convergent subsequence

questiontext.text: Dini's theorem states that if \(f_n\to f\) pointwise on a compact space \(K\), each \(f_n\) is continuous, \(f\) is continuous, and \((f_n)\) is monotone, then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) fₙ → f uniformly on K

Dini's theorem: pointwise monotone convergence of continuous functions to a continuous limit on a compact set implies uniform convergence.

answer.text: the convergence is only pointwise, not uniform

questiontext.text: The Gauss (Divergence) theorem relates a surface integral to a volume integral: for a smooth vector field \(\mathbf{F}\) and a region \(V\) with boundary \(\partial V\),

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) ∯_{∂V} F·n dS = ∭_V (∇·F) dV

Gauss's theorem (divergence theorem): the flux of F through the closed surface ∂V equals the volume integral of div F over V.

questiontext.text: To maximize \(f(x,y)\) subject to the constraint \(g(x,y)=0\), the Lagrange condition at a critical point is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) ∇f = λ∇g for some multiplier λ

Lagrange's method: at a constrained extremum, ∇f = λ∇g (the gradients are proportional), along with g(x,y)=0. The multiplier λ is the Lagrange multiplier.

questiontext.text: For a linear map \(T:V\to W\) between finite-dimensional vector spaces, the rank-nullity theorem states:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) dim V = dim(ker T) + dim(im T)

Rank-nullity: dim(ker T) + dim(im T) = dim(V). Here rank(T) = dim(im T) and nullity(T) = dim(ker T).

name.text: P08 Q21 - Gram matrix positive semidefinite

questiontext.text: The Gram matrix \(G_{ij}=\langle v_i,v_j\rangle\) of a set of vectors \(\{v_1,\ldots,v_n\}\) in an inner product space is always:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) positive semi-definite and Hermitian

The Gram matrix is always positive semi-definite (x*Gx = ‖Σxᵢvᵢ‖² ≥ 0) and Hermitian (G_{ij} = G̅_{ji}). It is positive definite iff the vectors are linearly independent.

questiontext.text: For a finite group \(G\), the class equation is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) |G| = |Z(G)| + Σ [G : C_G(gᵢ)]

The class equation partitions G into conjugacy classes: |G| = |Z(G)| + Σ_{conjugacy classes of size>1} |G|/|C_G(g)| where C_G(g) is the centralizer of g. Z(G) consists of singleton conjugacy classes.

questiontext.text: The orbit-stabilizer theorem states that for a group \(G\) acting on a set \(X\) and \(x\in X\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) |G| = |Orb(x)| × |Stab(x)|

Orbit-stabilizer theorem: |G| = |G·x| × |G_x| where G·x is the orbit and G_x = {g ∈ G : g·x = x} is the stabilizer of x. This is a special case of Lagrange's theorem.

questiontext.text: A holomorphic function \(f\) is conformal (angle-preserving) at a point \(z_0\) if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) f'(z₀) ≠ 0

Conformal maps preserve both angles and orientation. A holomorphic function is conformal precisely at points where f'(z₀) ≠ 0 (the derivative is nonzero).

name.text: P08 Q25 - Residue theorem higher order pole

questiontext.text: The residue of \(f(z)=\dfrac{e^z}{(z-1)^3}\) at \(z=1\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) e/2

Residue at a pole of order m: Res = (1/(m−1)!) lim_{z→1} d^{m-1}/dz^{m-1} [(z−1)^m f(z)]. Here m=3: Res = (1/2!) d²/dz² [e^z]|_{z=1} = e/2.

questiontext.text: A topological space \(X\) is locally compact if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) every point has a compact neighborhood

Local compactness: every point has a neighborhood whose closure is compact. ℝⁿ is locally compact but not compact; compact spaces are locally compact.

answer.text: every open cover has a finite subcover
every point has a compact neighborhood

name.text: P08 Q27 - Fourier series convergence pointwise

questiontext.text: Dirichlet's theorem on Fourier series states that if \(f\) is piecewise smooth and periodic, then the Fourier series converges at each point \(x\) to:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) [f(x+) + f(x-)]/2 (average of left and right limits)

Dirichlet's theorem: at each point of continuity, the Fourier series converges to f(x). At a jump discontinuity, it converges to the average of the left and right limits.

answer.text: does not converge at any point

name.text: P08 Q28 - Bounded variation and Stieltjes integral

questiontext.text: The Riemann-Stieltjes integral \(\int_a^b f\,dg\) exists if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) f is continuous and g has bounded variation

The Riemann-Stieltjes integral ∫f dg exists if f is continuous and g is of bounded variation on [a,b]. The value equals Σf(xᵢ)[g(xᵢ)−g(xᵢ₋₁)] in the limit.

answer.text: \(f\) is continuous and \(g\) has bounded variation

questiontext.text: A Poisson process with rate \(\lambda\) satisfies: the number of arrivals \(N(t)\) in \([0,t]\) has distribution:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) Poisson(λt)

N(t) ~ Poisson(λt): P(N(t)=k) = e^{-λt}(λt)^k/k!. The expected number of arrivals in [0,t] is λt.

questiontext.text: A statistic \(V(X)\) is said to be ancillary for parameter \(\theta\) if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) the distribution of V does not depend on θ

An ancillary statistic has a distribution free of the parameter θ. Example: the range of a normal sample is ancillary for the mean but not for the variance.

answer.text: the distribution of \(V\) does not depend on \(\theta\)

questiontext.text: The Cholesky decomposition of a symmetric positive definite matrix \(A\) gives \(A=LL^T\) where:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) L is a lower triangular matrix with positive diagonal entries

Cholesky decomposition: A = LLᵀ where L is lower triangular with positive diagonal entries. Exists and is unique for symmetric positive definite matrices. Useful for solving linear systems efficiently.

answer.text: \(L\) is lower triangular with positive diagonal entries

questiontext.text: In the QR decomposition \(A=QR\) of an \(m\times n\) matrix with \(m\geq n\), \(Q\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) m×m orthogonal (unitary) and R is m×n upper triangular

QR: Q is m×m orthogonal (columns are orthonormal), R is m×n upper triangular. Obtained via Gram-Schmidt. Used for solving least-squares problems.

answer.text: \(m\times m\) orthogonal and \(R\) is \(m\times n\) upper triangular
\(m\times n\) with orthonormal columns and \(R\) is \(n\times n\) symmetric

questiontext.text: The Poincaré inequality on a bounded domain \(\Omega\subset\mathbb{R}^n\) states that for \(u\in H_0^1(\Omega)\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) ∫_Ω u² dx ≤ C ∫_Ω |∇u|² dx

Poincaré inequality: for functions in H₀¹(Ω) (zero boundary conditions), ‖u‖_{L²} ≤ C‖∇u‖_{L²}. This is crucial in the existence theory for elliptic PDEs via Lax-Milgram.

name.text: P08 Q34 - PDE elliptic maximum principle

questiontext.text: The strong maximum principle for the Laplace equation \(\Delta u=0\) in a bounded domain \(\Omega\) states:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) if u achieves its maximum at an interior point, then u is constant

Strong maximum principle: a non-constant harmonic function in a connected domain cannot achieve its maximum (or minimum) at an interior point. Equivalently, the max/min are always attained on the boundary ∂Ω.

answer.text: if \(u\) achieves its maximum at an interior point of \(\Omega\), then \(u\) is constant on \(\Omega\)
\(u\) always achieves its maximum in the interior of \(\Omega\)
the maximum of \(u\) is always zero

questiontext.text: The Cauchy-Kowalevski theorem guarantees a local analytic solution to the Cauchy problem for a PDE if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) the data and coefficients are analytic and the initial surface is non-characteristic

Cauchy-Kowalevski theorem: if the PDE coefficients and initial data are real-analytic, and the initial surface is non-characteristic, then a unique local real-analytic solution exists.

answer.text: the PDE coefficients and initial data are real-analytic, and the initial surface is non-characteristic

questiontext.text: For a symmetric random walk on \(\mathbb{Z}\) (steps ±1 with equal probability 1/2), which of the following is TRUE?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) the walk returns to 0 with probability 1 (recurrent)

The symmetric random walk on ℤ is recurrent — it returns to the origin with probability 1 (Pólya's theorem). In ℤ³ and higher dimensions, the walk is transient.

answer.text: the walk is transient (returns to 0 with probability less than 1)
the walk is recurrent (returns to 0 with probability 1)
the walk converges to a limiting distribution
the walk reaches every integer in finite time with probability 0

questiontext.text: The Riesz-Fischer theorem states that \(L^2([a,b])\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) complete (a Hilbert space)

Riesz-Fischer: L²([a,b]) is complete — every Cauchy sequence in L² converges in L². This makes L²([a,b]) a Hilbert space. (Note: convergence is in norm, not pointwise.)

answer.text: complete (every Cauchy sequence converges in \(L^2\) norm) — a Hilbert space

questiontext.text: For \(1

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) L^q(μ) where 1/p + 1/q = 1

(L^p)* ≅ L^q for 1

questiontext.text: For an \(n\times n\) matrix \(A\), the relationship between the Frobenius norm \(\|A\|_F=\sqrt{\sum_{i,j}a_{ij}^2}\) and the spectral norm \(\|A\|_2\) (largest singular value) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) ‖A‖₂ ≤ ‖A‖_F ≤ √n ‖A‖₂

‖A‖₂ = σ₁ (largest singular value). ‖A‖_F = √(Σσᵢ²). Since σ₁ ≤ ‖A‖_F and ‖A‖_F = √(Σσᵢ²) ≤ √(n·σ₁²) = √n·σ₁, we get ‖A‖₂ ≤ ‖A‖_F ≤ √n·‖A‖₂.

name.text: P08 Q40 - Exponential family canonical form

questiontext.text: The Normal distribution \(N(\mu,\sigma^2)\) with known \(\sigma^2\) belongs to the exponential family. The natural (canonical) parameter is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) μ/σ²

For N(μ,σ²) with known σ², the density is f(x;μ) ∝ exp(μx/σ² − μ²/(2σ²)), so the natural parameter η = μ/σ² and T(x) = x is the sufficient statistic.

category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Model Paper 08/Part C - Advanced Mathematics

info.text: Part C: Advanced Mathematics (Q41–55). MSQ: +4, +2 partial, −1 wrong.

name.text: P08 Q41 MSQ - Stochastic processes martingales

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about martingales?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Definition of martingale. (2) TRUE: Optional stopping theorem. (3) TRUE: Martingale convergence theorem. (4) TRUE: Doob's L² inequality for martingales.

answer.text: A stochastic process \((M_n)\) is a martingale if \(E[M_{n+1}|\mathcal{F}_n]=M_n\) a.s.
For a martingale stopped at a bounded stopping time \(\tau\), \(E[M_\tau]=E[M_0]\) (optional stopping).
An \(L^2\)-bounded martingale converges almost surely and in \(L^2\) (martingale convergence theorem).
Doob's maximal inequality: \(P(\max_{k\leq n}M_k\geq\lambda)\leq E[M_n^+]/\lambda\) for a non-negative submartingale.

name.text: P08 Q42 MSQ - Cardinality and set theory

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about cardinalities of infinite sets?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: |ℕ| = ℵ₀ (countably infinite). (2) TRUE: Cantor's diagonal argument. (3) TRUE: |P(ℕ)| = |ℝ| = 2^ℵ₀. (4) TRUE: Cantor-Schröder-Bernstein.

answer.text: The set of natural numbers \(\mathbb{N}\) and the set of rational numbers \(\mathbb{Q}\) have the same cardinality \(\aleph_0\).
The set \(\mathbb{R}\) is uncountable (Cantor's diagonal argument).
The power set \(\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})\) has the same cardinality as \(\mathbb{R}\): \(|\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})|=2^{\aleph_0}=|\mathbb{R}|\).
The Cantor-Schröder-Bernstein theorem: if \(|A|\leq|B|\) and \(|B|\leq|A|\), then \(|A|=|B|\).

name.text: P08 Q43 MSQ - Analytic number theory

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about prime numbers and the prime number theorem?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: PNT: π(x)~x/ln x. (2) TRUE: Infinitely many primes (Euclid). (3) TRUE: Dirichlet's theorem on primes in AP. (4) TRUE: Twin prime conjecture is open.

answer.text: The prime number theorem: \(\pi(x)\sim x/\ln x\) as \(x\to\infty\) (where \(\pi(x)\) counts primes \(\leq x\)).
There are infinitely many prime numbers (Euclid's theorem).
Dirichlet's theorem: for \(\gcd(a,d)=1\), the arithmetic progression \(a, a+d, a+2d,\ldots\) contains infinitely many primes.
The twin prime conjecture (infinitely many pairs of primes \((p,p+2)\)) remains unproven.

name.text: P08 Q44 MSQ - Fixed point theorems

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about fixed point theorems?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Banach contraction. (2) TRUE: Brouwer on compact convex sets. (3) TRUE: Schauder for compact maps. (4) TRUE: Tarski for complete lattices.

answer.text: Banach contraction principle: a strict contraction on a complete metric space has a unique fixed point.
Brouwer: every continuous map from a compact convex set to itself has a fixed point.
Schauder: a continuous compact map from a convex closed bounded subset of a Banach space to itself has a fixed point.
Tarski: every monotone function on a complete lattice has a fixed point.

name.text: P08 Q45 MSQ - Differential equations power series

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about power series solutions of ODEs?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Ordinary point → power series solution. (2) TRUE: Frobenius method for regular singular points. (3) TRUE: Bessel equation has regular singular point at 0. (4) TRUE: At an ordinary point, the power series converges in the largest disk free of singularities.

answer.text: At an ordinary point, a linear ODE has two independent power series solutions.
At a regular singular point, the Frobenius method gives at least one solution of the form \(x^r\sum a_n x^n\).
The Bessel equation \(x^2y''+xy'+(x^2-n^2)y=0\) has a regular singular point at \(x=0\).
The radius of convergence of a power series solution around an ordinary point equals the distance to the nearest singularity.

name.text: P08 Q46 MSQ - Weak topology functional analysis

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about the weak topology on a Banach space \(X\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(4)

(1) TRUE: Weak convergence means f(xₙ)→f(x) for all f∈X*. (2) TRUE: Strong convergence implies weak convergence. (3) FALSE: In infinite-dimensional spaces, a weakly convergent sequence need NOT be norm convergent (e.g., Hilbert space eₙ→0 weakly but ‖eₙ‖=1). (4) TRUE: Banach-Alaoglu: the dual unit ball is weak* compact.

answer.text: \(x_n\xrightarrow{w} x\) (weakly) iff \(f(x_n)\to f(x)\) for every \(f\in X^*\).
In every infinite-dimensional Banach space, weak convergence implies norm convergence.
The closed unit ball of the dual \(X^*\) is compact in the weak* topology (Banach-Alaoglu theorem).

name.text: P08 Q47 MSQ - Differential geometry curvature

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about curvature of curves and surfaces?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Gauss-Bonnet. (2) TRUE: Gaussian curvature K = κ₁κ₂. (3) TRUE: Theorema Egregium (intrinsic). (4) TRUE: Constant negative curvature on hyperbolic plane.

answer.text: The Gaussian curvature \(K=\kappa_1\kappa_2\) (product of principal curvatures) is an intrinsic invariant (Gauss's Theorema Egregium).
Gaussian curvature is preserved by isometries (Theorema Egregium).
The hyperbolic plane has constant negative Gaussian curvature \(K=-1\).

name.text: P08 Q48 MSQ - Functional analysis closed graph

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about the Closed Graph Theorem?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Statement of CGT. (2) TRUE: Graph is closed iff xₙ→x and Txₙ→y implies y=Tx. (3) TRUE: CGT follows from OMT/Baire. (4) TRUE: Unbounded operators can have closed graphs (e.g., differentiation on appropriate domain).

answer.text: If \(T:X\to Y\) is a linear operator between Banach spaces and its graph is closed, then \(T\) is bounded (Closed Graph Theorem).
The graph of \(T\) is closed iff: \(x_n\to x\) and \(Tx_n\to y\) implies \(y=Tx\).
The Closed Graph Theorem follows from the Open Mapping Theorem and the Baire category theorem.
Unbounded operators (like differentiation on \(L^2\)) can still have closed graphs when defined on appropriate dense domains.

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about classical probability inequalities?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Markov's inequality. (2) TRUE: Chebyshev's inequality. (3) TRUE: Jensen's inequality. (4) TRUE: Chernoff bounds for sums of independent variables.

answer.text: Markov's inequality: \(P(X\geq a)\leq E[X]/a\) for non-negative \(X\) and \(a>0\).
Chernoff bounds give exponentially decaying tail probabilities for sums of independent bounded random variables.

name.text: P08 Q50 MSQ - Symplectic geometry Hamiltonian

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about Hamiltonian mechanics and symplectic geometry?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3)

(1) TRUE: Hamilton's equations. (2) TRUE: Liouville theorem — phase space volume preserved. (3) TRUE: Poisson bracket {H,H}=0 so H is conserved. (4) FALSE: Symplectic manifolds are always even-dimensional (the form ω is non-degenerate on even-dim manifolds).

answer.text: Hamilton's equations are \(\dot q_i=\partial H/\partial p_i\) and \(\dot p_i=-\partial H/\partial q_i\).
Liouville's theorem: Hamiltonian flow preserves phase space volume.
The Hamiltonian \(H\) is conserved along its own flow (\(\{H,H\}=0\)).

name.text: P08 Q51 MSQ - Abstract harmonic analysis

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about the Fourier analysis on locally compact abelian groups?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Characters (homomorphisms to T) form the Pontryagin dual. (2) TRUE: Pontryagin duality for LCA groups. (3) TRUE: Fourier inversion on ℝ. (4) TRUE: Plancherel on LCA groups.

answer.text: The Pontryagin dual \(\hat G\) of an LCA group \(G\) consists of continuous homomorphisms \(G\to\mathbb{T}\) (characters).
Pontryagin duality: \(\hat{\hat{G}}\cong G\) for any LCA group \(G\).
Fourier inversion holds on \(\mathbb{R}\): \(f(x)=\int\hat f(\xi)e^{2\pi ix\xi}\,d\xi\) (under appropriate conditions).
The Plancherel theorem extends to LCA groups: the Fourier transform is an isometry on \(L^2\).

name.text: P08 Q52 MSQ - Algebraic structures lattices

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about lattices as algebraic structures?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Lattice = partially ordered set with all finite meets and joins. (2) TRUE: Boolean algebra is a complemented distributive lattice. (3) TRUE: Modular lattices arise in group theory (subgroup lattices). (4) TRUE: The lattice of subspaces of a vector space is modular.

answer.text: A lattice is a partially ordered set in which every pair of elements has a least upper bound (join) and greatest lower bound (meet).
A Boolean algebra is a complemented distributive lattice.
The lattice of normal subgroups of a group is a modular lattice.
The lattice of subspaces of a vector space is modular.

name.text: P08 Q53 MSQ - Generalized functions and Schwartz space

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about the Schwartz space \(\mathcal{S}(\mathbb{R})\) and tempered distributions?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: S consists of rapidly decreasing smooth functions. (2) TRUE: Fourier transform maps S to S bijectively. (3) TRUE: Tempered distributions are continuous functionals on S. (4) TRUE: The Fourier transform extends to tempered distributions.

answer.text: The Schwartz space \(\mathcal{S}(\mathbb{R})\) consists of smooth functions that decay (along with all derivatives) faster than any polynomial.
The Fourier transform is a bijection on \(\mathcal{S}(\mathbb{R})\).
Tempered distributions are continuous linear functionals on \(\mathcal{S}(\mathbb{R})\).
The Fourier transform extends naturally to tempered distributions.

name.text: P08 Q54 MSQ - Combinatorics generating functions

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about generating functions?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: OGF of sequences. (2) TRUE: EGF used for counting permutations/labeled structures. (3) TRUE: Catalan numbers via OGF. (4) TRUE: Convolution of OGFs.

answer.text: The ordinary generating function (OGF) of a sequence \((a_n)\) is \(A(x)=\sum_{n\geq 0}a_n x^n\).
Exponential generating functions (EGFs) \(\sum a_n x^n/n!\) are natural for counting labeled structures.
The Catalan numbers \(C_n=\frac{1}{n+1}\binom{2n}{n}\) have generating function \(C(x)=\frac{1-\sqrt{1-4x}}{2x}\).
If \(A(x)\) and \(B(x)\) are OGFs of \((a_n)\) and \((b_n)\), then \(A(x)B(x)\) is the OGF of the convolution \(c_n=\sum_{k=0}^n a_k b_{n-k}\).

name.text: P08 Q55 MSQ - Functional calculus bounded operators

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about functional calculus for bounded self-adjoint operators \(A\) on a Hilbert space?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: f(A) is defined for continuous f on σ(A). (2) TRUE: Homomorphism property. (3) TRUE: ‖f(A)‖ = sup_{λ∈σ(A)} |f(λ)|. (4) TRUE: f(A)g(A)=g(A)f(A) for all continuous f,g.

answer.text: For a bounded self-adjoint operator \(A\), \(f(A)\) is well-defined for every continuous function \(f\) on the spectrum \(\sigma(A)\).
The map \(f\mapsto f(A)\) is a \(*\)-algebra homomorphism from \(C(\sigma(A))\) to \(B(H)\).
For any two continuous functions \(f,g\) on \(\sigma(A)\), \(f(A)\) and \(g(A)\) commute.
